elements.sent-2411-003: The elastic net penalty is frequently employed in high-dimensional statistics for parameter regression and variable selection.
elements.sent-2411-004: It is particularly beneficial compared to lasso when the number of predictors greatly surpasses the number of observations.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, empirical evidence has shown that the $\ell_q$-norm penalty (where $0 < q < 1$) often provides better regression compared to the $\ell_1$-norm penalty, demonstrating enhanced robustness in various scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this paper, we explore a generalized elastic net model that employs a $\ell_r$-norm (where $r \geq 1$) in loss function to accommodate various types of noise, and employs a $\ell_q$-norm (where $0 < q < 1$) to replace the $\ell_1$-norm in elastic net penalty.
elements.sent-2411-007: Theoretically, we establish the computable lower bounds for the nonzero entries of the generalized first-order stationary points of the proposed generalized elastic net model.
elements.sent-2411-008: For implementation, we develop two efficient algorithms based on the locally Lipschitz continuous $\epsilon$-approximation to $\ell_q$-norm.
elements.sent-2411-009: The first algorithm employs an alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM), while the second utilizes a proximal majorization-minimization method (PMM), where the subproblems are addressed using the semismooth Newton method (SNN).
elements.sent-2411-010: We also perform extensive numerical experiments with both simulated and real data, showing that both algorithms demonstrate superior performance.
elements.sent-2411-011: Notably, the PMM-SSN is efficient than ADMM, even though the latter provides a simpler implementation.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper, we mainly consider a high-dimensional sparse linear regression model.
elements.sent-2411-013: Suppose that the data set has $n$ observations with $p$ predictors.
elements.sent-2411-014: Let $y\in \mathbb{R ^n$ be the response and $X:=[X_1,X_2,...,X_p]\in\mathbb{R ^{n\times p}$ be the sample matrix, where $X_i\in\mathbb{R ^n$ for $i=1,...,p$ are the predictors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: We assume that the following linear relation is data generation process: \begin{equation}y= X\underline\beta+\varepsilon,\end{equation} where $\underline\beta\in\mathbb{R ^p$ is the model fitting procedure produces the vector of coefficient, and $\varepsilon\in \mathbb{R ^n$ is the noise, which is not limited to a specific type of noise.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Without loss of generality, we assume that the above data has been centralized.
elements.sent-2411-017: In high-dimensional setting, the number of predictors $p$ is much larger than the number of observations $n$ and that $\underline\beta$ is known to be sparse a priori.
elements.sent-2411-018: Under this assumption, penalty techniques are often employed to manage overfitting and facilitate variable selection.
elements.sent-2411-019: A typical method called Lasso \cite{lasso2,lasso adds a $\ell_1$-norm penalty to the ordinary least square loss function, which penalizes the absolute size of the coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Subsequently, the estimation model is described as following: \begin{equation}\min_{\beta\in R^p} \frac{1}{2}\|X\beta-y\|^2_2+\lambda\|\beta\|_1,\end{equation} where $\|\beta\|_1$ is $\ell_1$-norm (named Lasso) and $\lambda>0$ is a tuning parameter.
elements.sent-2411-021: In view of the good characteristics of the $\ell_1$-norm, the Lasso does both continuous shrinkage and automatic variable selection simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-022: Although Lasso has received extensive attention in different application fields, it has some limitations.
elements.sent-2411-023: For the case of $p\gg n $ and grouped variables situation, the Lasso can only select at most $n$ variables before it saturates, and it lacks the ability to reveal the grouping information \cite{net1,net3,net2 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: As a result, Zou et al \cite{net2 proposed an elastic net penalty approach for both estimation and variable selection: \begin{equation}\label{net0} \min_{\beta\in \mathbb{R}^p} \frac{1}{2}\|X\beta-y\|^2_2+\lambda_2\|\beta\|^2_2+\lambda_1\|\beta\|_1,\end{equation} where $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are two non-negative tuning parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Similar to Lasso, the elastic net penalty simultaneously does automatic variable selection and continuous shrinkage, and it can select groups of correlated variables \cite{net2 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In fact, the $\ell_1$-norm is a loose approximation function which leads to an over-regularized problem.
elements.sent-2411-027: Motivated by the fact that the $\ell_q$-norm penalty with $0 < q < 1$ can usually induce better sparse regression results than $\ell_1$-norm penalty \cite{lq1 , with improved robustness \cite{lq2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: A natural improvement is the using of a $\ell_q$-norm to replace the $\ell_1$-norm in elastic net penalty, see also \cite{lqnet : \begin{equation}\label{net1} \min_{\beta\in \mathbb{R}^p} \frac{1}{2}\|X\beta-y\|^2_2+\lambda_2\|\beta\|^2_2+\lambda_1\|\beta\|^q_q,\end{equation} where $\|\beta\|_q=(\sum_{i=1 ^p|\beta_i|^q)^{1/q}$ is a $\ell_1$-norm.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: It is important to note that the error vector $\varepsilon$ may not approach zero and may not follow a normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-030: Although least squares estimation is computationally attractive, it relies on knowing the standard deviation of Gaussian noise, which can be challenging to estimate in high-dimensional settings.
elements.sent-2411-031: A key question is whether we can extend the model (\ref{net1 ) to accommodate different types of noise, beyond the least squares loss function.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This question is important because when noise does not follow a normal distribution, alternative loss functions may be more robust.
elements.sent-2411-034: The square-root loss \cite{l21 removes the need to know or pre-estimate the deviation and can achieve near-oracle rates of convergence under some appropriate conditions \cite{l22,l23,l24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: It also has been shown that $\ell_{\infty}$-norm loss performs better when deal with the uniformly distributed noise and quantization error \cite{l33,l31,l32 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore, in this paper, we mainly concentrate on a generalized elastic net problem with a $\ell_r$-norm (where $r\geq 1$) loss function to result in the following model \begin{equation}\label{lp} \min_{\beta\in \mathbb{R}^p} \Big\{ F(\beta):=\|X\beta-y\|_r+\lambda_2\|\beta\|^2_2+\lambda_1\|\beta\|^q_q\Big\},\end{equation} where $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2 \geq 0$ are tuning parameters.
elements.sent-2411-037: We note that the $\ell_r$-norm, associated with the parameter $r$, is assumed to have a strongly semismooth proximal mapping.
elements.sent-2411-038: This assumption regarding $\ell_r$-norm is mild and often encountered.
elements.sent-2411-039: For instance, the proximal mappings associated with $\ell_1$-, $\ell_2$- and $\ell_\infty$-norms are all strongly semismooth.
elements.sent-2411-040: These specific norms are commonly employed in optimization problems that involve loss functions for addressing various types of noise \cite{l33,l34 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The selection of $r$ is suitable for different types of noise, for instance, $r = 1$ is ideal for heavy-tailed or heterogeneous noise, $r = 2$ is appropriate when the standard deviation of Gaussian noise is unknown, and $r = \infty$ fits scenarios involving uniformly distributed noise.
elements.sent-2411-042: It is known that $ \ell_q$ for $0 < q < 1$ is nonsmooth and nonconvex, and it may not even be Lipschitz continuous \cite{np1,np2 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: To address this issue, several effective iterative reweighted algorithms regarding $\ell_1$- and $\ell_2$-norms have been proposed \cite{ir1, ir3, ir2, lqnet .}
elements.sent-2411-044: For instance, Lu \cite{lu presented a Lipschitz continuous $\epsilon$-approximation of the $\ell_q$-norm and empirically demonstrated that this new approach generally outperforms current iterative reweighted methods.}
elements.sent-2411-045: These algorithms rely on least absolute deviation or least squares approximations of the $\ell_q$-norm, with the goal of finding an approximate solution to (\ref{net1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-046: The successful execution of these algorithms primarily arises from the smoothness of the least squares loss term in (\ref{net1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, the nonconvex nonsmooth properties of $\ell_q$-norm, combined with the nonsmooth nature of the $\ell_r$-norm loss function, make the numerical solving of model (\ref{lp ) considerably more difficult.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Drawing inspiration from the work of Lu \cite{lu , this paper transforms the generalized elastic net model \eqref{lp into a convex yet nonsmooth iterative reweighted $\ell_1$-norm minimization by utilizing an $\epsilon$-approximation.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: We begin by defining the generalized first-order stationary point of (\ref{lp ), which helps us establish lower bounds and identify its local minimizers.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Subsequently, we demonstrate that any accumulation point obtained from the sequence generated by the approximation technique converges to a generalized first-order stationary point, provided that the approximation parameter $\epsilon$ remains below a certain threshold value.
elements.sent-2411-051: It is crucial to highlight that the estimation model obtained from this approximation is convex but non-smooth.
elements.sent-2411-052: As a result, we develop two efficient practical methods to fully utilize the underlying structures of the convex approximate minimization model.
elements.sent-2411-053: The first algorithm is the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM), known for its ease of implementation.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, ADMM is still a first-order method, which may restrict its efficiency when tackling problems with high accuracy requirements.
elements.sent-2411-055: In light of this, we also employ a novel proximal majorization minimization (PMM) approach, see \cite{l24 , to solve the subproblem through its dual using a highly efficient semismooth Newton (SSN) method.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Finally, we perform a series of numerical experiments that highlight the remarkable superiority of the generalized elastic net model (\ref{lp ) and show that both of our proposed algorithms are efficient.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-058: In Section \ref{preli , we summarize key concepts and address the $\epsilon$-approximation problem.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Section \ref{comp outlines our motivation and constructs the algorithms, including their convergence results.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Section \ref{num4 presents numerical results to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Finally, we conclude this paper in Section \ref{con5 .}

elements.sent-2411-302: In this paper, we introduced a generalized elastic net model for sparse linear regression that utilizes the $\ell_r$-norm as the loss function and incorporates the $\ell_q$-norm in the elastic net penalty.
elements.sent-2411-303: We established the generalized first-order stationary point for this generalized elastic net model and subsequently derived lower bounds for its non-zero entries and local minimizers.
elements.sent-2411-304: Furthermore, utilizing the $\epsilon$-approximation function $h_{u_{\epsilon }$ from Lu \cite{lu , we demonstrated that any accumulation point of the sequences generated by these methods is a generalized first-order stationary point.}}
elements.sent-2411-305: For practical implementation, we proposed two efficient algorithms within an iterative reweighted framework: one is the easily implementable first-order algorithm ADMM, and the other is the faster PMM-SSN algorithm, which leverages second-order information.
elements.sent-2411-306: To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms, we conducted a series of numerical experiments using both simulated data and a real dataset.
elements.sent-2411-307: We compared their performance against the state-of-the-art algorithm, IAGENR-Lq.
elements.sent-2411-308: The results indicated that both algorithms are effective in addressing high-dimensional sparse linear regression problems and exhibited strong robustness across different types of noise.
elements.sent-2411-309: Our findings revealed that while ADMM performed well in numerical experiments due to its straightforward implementation, the PMM-SSN demonstrated superior performance in tackling complex problems, particularly when second-order information plays a crucial role in improving solution accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-310: Consequently, for high-dimensional sparse linear regression problems that demand higher precision, the PMM-SSN may offer a more effective choice.

elements.sent-2411-314: We theoretically prove that the local minimizer of the proposed model is a generalized first-order stationary point, and then derive the computable lower bounds for the nonzero entries of this generalized stationary point.
elements.sent-2411-315: For the implementation, we utilize an iterative reweighted framework that leverages the locally Lipschitz continuous $\epsilon$-approximation, and subsequently propose two optimization algorithms.
